title: Translation unit and main

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Translation unit and main; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A hosted C program starts through a conforming main definition, returns an integer termination status, and receives arguments only through a declared supported main signature.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A minimal hosted program declares int main(void), prints one line, and returns zero after successful completion. Your goal is to write and explain a minimal conforming hosted C program. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: pf computing execution; pf tools terminal editor. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: A hosted C program starts through a conforming main definition, returns an integer termination status, and receives arguments only through a declared supported main signature. This rule is applied to concrete C source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: Freestanding implementations can define a different entry environment, and operating systems interpret status values beyond portable success and failure conventions differently. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: C programs may use void main because ignoring a result is equivalent to returning a valid termination status.

includes: Identify declarations, definitions, statements and the hosted program entry point.
Write and explain a minimal conforming hosted C program.
excludes: Freestanding implementations can define a different entry environment, and operating systems interpret status values beyond portable success and failure conventions differently.

outcomes: Write and explain a minimal conforming hosted C program.

examples.title: Translation unit and main: defined case
examples.situation: A minimal hosted program declares int main(void), prints one line, and returns zero after successful completion.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: A minimal hosted program declares int main(void), prints one line, and returns zero after successful completion.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: For a portable hosted program, define int main(void) or a supported argument form and return an integer status; void main is not a portable hosted definition.
examples.result: The defined result follows because For a portable hosted program, define int main(void) or a supported argument form and return an integer status; void main is not a portable hosted definition.

examples.title: Translation unit and main: boundary case
examples.situation: A command-line program validates argc before reading argv[1] and returns a nonzero status when the required argument is absent.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: A command-line program validates argc before reading argv[1] and returns a nonzero status when the required argument is absent.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: Freestanding implementations can define a different entry environment, and operating systems interpret status values beyond portable success and failure conventions differently.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: C programs may use void main because ignoring a result is equivalent to returning a valid termination status.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Write and explain a minimal conforming hosted C program.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: C programs may use void main because ignoring a result is equivalent to returning a valid termination status.
misconceptions.correction: For a portable hosted program, define int main(void) or a supported argument form and return an integer status; void main is not a portable hosted definition.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete C rules, explain why this belief fails: C programs may use void main because ignoring a result is equivalent to returning a valid termination status.

analogy.example: A manually managed workshop can model Translation unit and main: every tool, address, buffer, and acquired resource has an explicit valid range and owner.
usefulFor: Making low-level state, bounds, representation, ownership, and cleanup responsibilities concrete
limitations: C abstract-machine rules, undefined behavior, pointer provenance, translation, and implementation variation are stricter than a physical workshop analogy.
Freestanding implementations can define a different entry environment, and operating systems interpret status values beyond portable success and failure conventions differently.

recap.summary: You should now be able to write and explain a minimal conforming hosted C program. Keep this boundary in view: Freestanding implementations can define a different entry environment, and operating systems interpret status values beyond portable success and failure conventions differently.
